content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
SUPERSEDED
Practices Cornmission IN RART
March 27, 
■990
17)15
`c)`ll)
Honorable Leo Mccarthy
Lieutenant Governor
State of california
State Capitol, Suite 1114
Sacramento, cA 958 f4
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-90-163
Dear Lieutenant Governor Mccarthy:
You have requested advice regarding the gpplication of provi-
sions of the Political 
Reform Act (the 'Act")a with respect to the
use of your name and photograph in adverti'sements by a private,
nonprof it, educational institution.
This letter presents a difficult 
question concerning
application of the contribution lirnits of Proposition 73. we have
giien "o.rs"rvative advj,ce, and will provide a copy of this letter
fo the commission for review at an upcoming commission meeting.
OUESTIONS
I. 
Would the use of your name and tikeness in the advertise-
rnent violate the mass rnailing provisions of Section 89001 of the
Act?
2. would the use of your name and likeness in advertisernents
paid for by the University of san Francisco be considered a
contribution to you by the university?
CONCLUSIONS
1. The use of your name and photograph in the advertisernent
would not violate the mass rnailing provisions of the Act, because
the advertisement will not be sent at public expense.
1 co.r...rr"nt code Sections 8l-ooo-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. 
Comrnission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18ooo, et seq. AIl references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street,Suitc 800 ● P.O.Box 807 0 Sacramento CA 95804´ 0807 ● (916)322´ 5660


Our File No. A-9O-153
Page 2
2. we conservatively advise that the proposed advertisements
paid for by the University of san Francisco would be considered an
in-kind contribution to you by the university.
'FACTS
The University of San Francisco, a private, nonprofit
institution, 
has requested perruission to use your name, title 
and
photograph in advertisenents pronoting a program for professional
stuaies. 
Based upon conversations with your staff, it is our
understanding that no public funds will be used to distribute the
advertisernent. we also understand that no costs of design,
production or printing in excess of $50 will be paid with public
iunds. However, the university does reguire that you siqn a
release before your name and photograph may be used in their
advertisement. You wish to know if this use of your name and
photograph would violate the mass nailing provisions of the Act or
it it mignt be considered a contribution to you from the
univers ity.
ANALYS I S
Mass Ma i l incr
Section 89001 of the Act provides that rrno newsletter or
other mass nailing shall be sent at public expense.t' A mass nail-
ing is tlefi.ned as t\,ro hundred or nore substantially similar pieces
of mail. 
(Section a2o4]-.5.) Regulation 1.89012 (copy enclosed)
clarifies that the purpose of Section 89001 is to prevent elected
officers frorn using public funds to send newsletters and other
rnass mailinqs at puUlic expense. It provides that a mass mailing
nay not be ient at public expense if it contains the name, office,
photograph or any other reference to an elected officer affiliated
witn tne agency producing or sending the mailing and is prepared
or sent in cooperatj,on, consultation, coordination or concert with
the elected officer. 
(Regnrlation l-8901(a).)
If public funds are not used to pay costs of design, produc-
tion, printing, or distribution, 
the rnass mail-ing restrictions 
do
not appty. Furthermore, Regulation 18901 provides that, if the
costs of distribution are not paid with public funds, and if no
rnore than $50 of public funds are used to pay costs of design,
production or printing, the mailing is not considered to be sent
at public expense. (Regulation 18901(a) (3) . )
2 A revis"d version of Regulation 1890I was adopted by the con-
mission on December 13, 1989. A statutorily required public coto-
rnent period expired January 17, 1990. Although the revised ver-
sion of the regulation is still 
in the ruLemaking process, it
represents the policy of the Corunission. Therefore, $re are advis-
ing agencies that they nay rely on it. 
The advice contained in
this letter is based upon the revised version of Regulation 18901.


Our File No. A-90-163
Page 3
Since no public funds will be used to distribute the
advertisement, and no costs of desiqn, production and printing
exceeding $50 r^Iill be paid with public funds, the proposed
advertisement will not be considered to be sent at public expense.
Therefore, the advertisement -wilI not violate the mass mailing
provisions .of the Act.
Contr ibution
Since the advertisement will be produced and paid for by a
private university, you have asked whether it would be considered
an in-kind contribution to You.
A contribution is any nonetary or nonmonetary payment rnade
for political 
purposes without ful1 and adequate consideration.
(Seclion 82015; Regulation 782L5, copy enctosed.) A payrnent is
rnade for political 
purposes if it is received by or made at the
behest of a candidate, unless it is clear from the surrounding
circumstances that the payment was received or made for personal
purposes unrelated to his or her status as a candidate or elected
otficer. 
(Regulation 18215(a)(2).) A pa]4lrent is rnade at the
behest of a cindidate it if is made in cooperation, consultation,
coordination, or concert rrith the candidate. (Regulation
18215(b).) i'or purposes of the Act, an incunbent elected officer
is considered a rrcandidate. rr (section 82oo7.)
I{e have previously advised that a crime prevention brochure
which included the photograph, name and title 
of an elected of-
ficer and which was to be distributed in the elected officer's
district 
constituted an in-kind contribution to the elected of-
ficer. 
(Roos Advice Letter A-85-057, coPY enclosed. )
The advertisement at issue here will be produced with your
cooperation. It will include your photograph, name and tit1e. 
As
lieutenant governor, the entire state is your ttdistrict.tt 
You are
currentfy a candidate for reelection and we are within three
nonths of the primary election. 
Based upon all of these factors,
we cannot say that it is clear that the proposed advertisenents
are unrelated to your status as a candidate or elected officer.
Therefore, we conservatively advise that they would be considered
an in-kind contribution to you from the university and subject to
the contribution linits of the Act. (Section 85300-85307.)


Our FiIe No. A-90-163
Page 4
If you have any questions regarding this matter, you may
contact me at (915) 322-5904.
S incerely ,
Kathryn E. Donovan
General counsel
忙
By: Margaret w. Ellison
CounseI, Legal Division
KED/MWE/aa
EncloSures
